channel.title: Science - Latest - Google News
channel.copyright: Copyright © 2026 Google. All rights reserved. This XML feed is made available solely for the purpose of rendering Google News results within a personal feed reader for personal, non-commercial use. Any other use of the feed is expressly prohibited. By accessing this feed or using these results in any manner whatsoever, you agree to be bound by the foregoing restrictions.

item.title: Some tropical butterfly species have unlocked an evolutionary way to live longer - CNN
item.description:
1. Some tropical butterfly species have unlocked an evolutionary way to live longer  CNN
  2. Evolution of increased longevity and slowed ageing in a genus of tropical butterfly  Nature
  3. The Butterfly Longevity Diet  The Atlantic
  4. Butterflies may hold clues to longer lives  NPR
  5. Tropical butterfly defies aging, lives 348 days and reveals longevity clues  mezha.net

item.title: 'No one thought it was going to be possible.' A space telescope is falling out of space. This is NASA's daring plan to save it. - Space
item.description:
1. 'No one thought it was going to be possible.' A space telescope is falling out of space. This is NASA's daring plan to save it.  Space
  2. A bold satellite rescue mission came together in record time, but will it work?  Ars Technica
  3. Aircraft Carrying Swift Boost Satellite Takes off From NASA Wallops  NASA Science (.gov)
  4. Northrop Grumman’s Pegasus Rocket to Power Unprecedented NASA Observatory Rescue  Northrop Grumman
  5. NASA's Swift Boost Mission Will Launch Later This Month To Rescue A Falling Telescope  Engadget

item.description:

  4. In 2028, Americans will fly to Titan - Saturn's moon, where helium will be extracted  Inbox.lv
  5. New Study Assesses Titan's Resources and their Potential Uses  Universe Today

item.title: On 5 March 1982, the Soviet Venera 14 lander reached the surface of Venus, ejected the protective cap from its camera lens, photographed the surrounding terrain — and then lowered its mechanical soil-testing arm directly onto the discarded lens cap, returni - 19FortyFive
item.description:
1. On 5 March 1982, the Soviet Venera 14 lander reached the surface of Venus, ejected the protective cap from its camera lens, photographed the surrounding terrain — and then lowered its mechanical soil-testing arm directly onto the discarded lens cap, returni  19FortyFive
  2. Venus’ Strange Rotation Was Likely Triggered By A High Velocity Moon-Sized Impactor  Universe Today
  3. Venus' retrograde rotation was caused by a moon impact  Universe Space Tech

item.title: Watch: Venus disappears behind the moon in stunning timelapse of rare, daytime lunar occultation - FOX Weather
item.description:

item.title: Antarctica is offering 30 to 50 years' worth of warning on sea level rise, models suggest - Phys.org
item.description:
1. Antarctica is offering 30 to 50 years' worth of warning on sea level rise, models suggest  Phys.org
  2. Scientists say the West Antarctic Ice Sheet could collapse with very little additional warming and the four metres of sea level rise that would follow cannot be stopped once it begins  Space Daily
  3. Emergent decadal predictability in Antarctic contribution to sea-level rise  Nature
  4. Vanderford Glacier, Credit: Monash SAEF (IMAGE)  EurekAlert!

item.description:

  4. NASA picks Eric Schmidt’s rocket company for Mars mission, setting up a race with SpaceX  TechCrunch
  5. A private company will build and launch NASA's next Mars orbiter in 2028 — and it's not SpaceX  Space

item.description:

  3. NASA’s Voyager 1 probe is about to reach a distance once thought unimaginable in space  MSN

item.title: In 1908, something exploded above the remote Siberian forest of Tunguska with enough force to flatten roughly 2,000 square kilometres of trees — and more than a century later, no impact crater has ever been found, because the object, probably a small aster - Space Daily

item.title: 'A completely different story': 300 million-year-old fossils reveal the first vertebrate land dwellers weren't what we thought, researchers claim - Live Science
item.description:
1. 'A completely different story': 300 million-year-old fossils reveal the first vertebrate land dwellers weren't what we thought, researchers claim  Live Science
  2. Earth’s First Land Animals May Never Have Been Amphibian-Like After All  Sci.News
  3. Baby crocodile-like fossils just blew up a long-held evolution theory  Popular Science
  4. Fossilized babies of ancient crocodile-like predators uproot understanding of how animals adapted to the land  Phys.org
  5. How New Fossil Discoveries Just Rewrote the History of Land Life  Yahoo

item.title: Humans May Soon Be Able to Regrow Body Parts—Including Fingers and Limbs—Thanks to a Groundbreaking Serum - Yahoo
item.description:
1. Humans May Soon Be Able to Regrow Body Parts—Including Fingers and Limbs—Thanks to a Groundbreaking Serum  Yahoo

  4. Mammal Tissue Regeneration Activated: FGF2 and BMP2 Override Scarring in Mice  Tech Times
  5. Scientists Discover Gene That One Day Could Help Humans Regrow Lost Limbs  ColombiaOne.com

item.title: The reason no human being has walked on the Moon in more than half a century is not that humanity lost the capability to get there — it is that humanity lost the reason, in the moment the Cold War goal of beating the Soviet Union to the lunar surface had finall - Space Daily
item.description: The reason no human being has walked on the Moon in more than half a century is not that humanity lost the capability to get there — it is that humanity lost the reason, in the moment the Cold War goal of beating the Soviet Union to the lunar surface had finall  Space Daily

item.description: NASA's Parker Solar Probe flew in and out of the solar corona and found a source of high-energy particles that no existing model had predicted  Space Daily

item.title: Enceladus, a tiny Saturnian moon only about 500 kilometres wide, is actively venting water vapour and ice grains into space from a salty ocean hidden beneath its crust — and modelling suggests that ocean may have remained warm and chemically active for - Space Daily

item.title: An exoplanet with a daytime temperature hot enough to vaporize iron has methane on its nightside because of an atmospheric circulation that should not be able to exist at that heat - Space Daily
item.description:
1. An exoplanet with a daytime temperature hot enough to vaporize iron has methane on its nightside because of an atmospheric circulation that should not be able to exist at that heat  Space Daily
  2. A Hot Jupiter’s Cloudy Mornings and Clear Evenings Provide Clues to Its Chemistry  eos.org
  3. James Webb Spots Something Strange Between Day and Night on an Alien Planet  SciTechDaily
  4. Asymmetric weather pattern reveals exoplanet’s true atmosphere  Physics World
  5. James Webb reveals a hellish exoplanet where dawn and dusk have wildly different chemistry  Yahoo

item.description:

  4. Denisovan DNA still switches genes on and off in humans  Earth.com
  5. Study finds Denisovan DNA still helping people in South Pacific region fight viruses today  MSN

item.title: The tallest volcano in the solar system is so wide that from most places on its slopes, you would never see a dramatic summit rising ahead of you — Olympus Mons is broader than some countries, and its gentle flanks curve away with Mars long before the mou - Space Daily
item.description:
1. The tallest volcano in the solar system is so wide that from most places on its slopes, you would never see a dramatic summit rising ahead of you — Olympus Mons is broader than some countries, and its gentle flanks curve away with Mars long before the mou  Space Daily
  2. The Largest Volcanoes Ever Found Beyond Earth  WorldAtlas

item.title: In late 2023, Voyager 1 began sending unreadable data from interstellar space. NASA engineers eventually traced the fault to corrupted memory in one of the probe’s computers, rewrote part of its 46-year-old software, transmitted the fix across more than 24 bil - Space Daily
item.description: In late 2023, Voyager 1 began sending unreadable data from interstellar space. NASA engineers eventually traced the fault to corrupted memory in one of the probe’s computers, rewrote part of its 46-year-old software, transmitted the fix across more than 24 bil  Space Daily

item.description:

  2. Sun may spare Earth and Mars, as study reverses course on death of our planet  WION
  3. Complex life on Earth may last 500 million years longer than expected  New Scientist
  4. The real reason Earth escapes the dying sun gravity  The News International
  5. Earth might actually survive sun’s fiery death as new study overturns decades-old forecast  Interesting Engineering

item.description:

  4. Cockroaches aren't just cockroaches: Their DNA contains traces of a 150-million-year-old bacterial partner  The Times of India

item.title: The longest continuous spaceflight in history — 437 days aboard the Russian Mir space station, completed by cosmonaut Valeri Polyakov in 1995 — was specifically conducted to prove that a human being could survive a round-trip mission to Mars, in a piece - Space Daily
item.description: The longest continuous spaceflight in history — 437 days aboard the Russian Mir space station, completed by cosmonaut Valeri Polyakov in 1995 — was specifically conducted to prove that a human being could survive a round-trip mission to Mars, in a piece  Space Daily

item.title: Point Nemo sits so far from any coastline that the closest humans to it are often the astronauts aboard the International Space Station passing overhead, not anyone standing on land. - Space Daily
item.description: Point Nemo sits so far from any coastline that the closest humans to it are often the astronauts aboard the International Space Station passing overhead, not anyone standing on land.  Space Daily
